group.explanation: Each word contains the letters of a metal: IRON, COPPER, LEAD, TIN (within PLATINUM).

errors: STIRON contains IRON — plausible but 'STIRON' is not a real English word, making it an invented/fake word.
SCOPPER contains COPPER — 'SCOPPER' is not a standard common English word.
BLEAD contains LEAD — 'BLEAD' is not a real English word.
PLATINUM contains TIN — this works, but the other three words are fabricated, making the group unusable.
The mechanism requires real, common English words hiding the metal; inventing words to fit is a disqualifying flaw.

group.category: Remove the last letter to get a color
group.explanation: Drop the final letter to get RED, BLUE, PINK, TAN.

errors: REDO → RED works cleanly.
BLUES → BLUE works cleanly.
PINKY → PINK works cleanly.
TANS → TAN works cleanly.
However, REDO dropping the last letter gives RED, but REDO is a common word meaning 'do again' — the transformation is valid mechanically.

The real issue: 'Remove the last letter to get a body part' already exists as a verified group (drop_last_letter_body_part). This group is a direct renamed repeat of that mechanism with a different category (color instead of body part). Per rules, renamed repeats of the same mechanism family are borderline, but the concept_key is distinct (drop_last_letter_color vs drop_last_letter_body_part).

Actually on closer review, the mechanism is identical to the existing verified group 'Remove the last letter to get a body part' — just swapping the target category. This is a renamed repeat and should be rejected to avoid puzzle overlap and staleness.
Additionally REDO→RED: REDO ends in O, removing gives RED ✓. BLUES→BLUE ✓. PINKY→PINK ✓. TANS→TAN ✓. Mechanically all four work, but the concept is a direct clone of an existing verified group.

group.explanation: Each sounds like a body part: muscle, hare->hair (hair itself), waist->waist, navel.

errors: MUSSEL → MUSCLE: valid homophone of a body part ✓.
HAIR: HAIR itself IS a body part — it is not a homophone OF a body part, it IS the body part. This breaks the rule; the word must sound like a body part but not be the body part itself.
WAIST → WASTE: WAIST is the body part, not a homophone of one. Same problem as HAIR — WAIST is the actual body part spelling.
NAVAL → NAVEL: valid homophone ✓.
Two of the four words (HAIR, WAIST) are the actual body-part words, not homophones of them. The category claims they are homophones, which is false for these two.
